Scaled dot-product attention: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(Q K^T / {{c1::sqrt(d_k)}}) V
column2: The scale keeps logit variance ~1 so softmax doesn't saturate.

column1: Attention scores are divided by 1/sqrt(d_k) because a dot product of d_k unit-variance terms has variance {{c1::d_k}} and standard deviation {{c2::sqrt(d_k)}}.
A transformer block has about {{c1::12}} d^2 params: attention {{c2::4}} d^2 (Q,K,V,O) and MLP {{c3::8}} d^2.
FLOPs per token: forward ~ {{c1::2N}}, backward ~ {{c2::4N}}, so training ~ {{c3::6N}} for an N-parameter model.
The gradient of softmax cross-entropy w.r.t. logits is {{c1::softmax(z) - onehot(y)}}.
Self-attention has time complexity {{c1::O(T^2 d)}} and memory {{c2::O(T^2)}} in sequence length T.
Modern LLMs use {{c1::pre}}-norm, computing x + Sublayer(LN(x)) for a clean identity residual path.
RoPE encodes {{c1::relative}} position inside the {{c2::QK dot product}} and adds {{c3::no}} extra parameters.
The softmax stability trick subtracts {{c1::max(x)}} before exp; it is {{c2::shift-invariant}} and prevents fp16 {{c3::overflow}}.
Compute, parameters, and tokens are tied by C = {{c1::6ND}}.
column2: About 6 FLOPs per parameter per token.

column1: Chinchilla-optimal scaling uses about {{c1::20}} tokens per parameter.
AdamW mixed-precision memory per param: weights {{c1::2}} + grads {{c2::2}} + optimizer {{c3::12}} = {{c4::16}} bytes.
Full fine-tuning a 7B model in bf16 + AdamW needs about {{c1::112}} GB (14 + 14 + 84) before activations.
bf16 keeps fp32's {{c1::8}}-bit exponent, so it rarely overflows and needs no {{c2::loss scaling}}.
Perplexity = {{c1::e^L}}, where L is the cross-entropy loss in nats.
With D ~ 20N at fixed compute, the optimal size is N ~ {{c1::sqrt(C/120)}}.
The factor 6 in C = 6ND splits into forward ~ {{c1::2}} and backward ~ {{c2::4}} FLOPs per param per token.
LoRA learns a low-rank update dW = {{c1::BA}}; for a d x d matrix it trains {{c2::2dr}} params, a ratio of {{c3::2r/d}}.
LoRA forward: h = {{c1::W0 x}} + (alpha/r) {{c2::B A x}}; A is {{c3::random}}-init, B is {{c4::zero}}-init.
The reward-model (Bradley-Terry) loss is {{c1::-log sigmoid(r(x,y_w) - r(x,y_l))}}.
The DPO implicit reward is {{c1::beta log(pi / pi_ref)}}.
column2: Substituted into Bradley-Terry to get the DPO loss.

column1: In the DPO loss, beta controls {{c1::preference strength vs drift}} and pi_ref {{c2::anchors}} the policy to the reference.
QLoRA's three tricks: {{c1::NF4}} 4-bit quantization, {{c2::double quantization}}, and {{c3::paged optimizers}}.
7B fine-tuning memory: full ~ {{c1::112}} GB, LoRA ~ {{c2::15}} GB, QLoRA ~ {{c3::5}} GB.
In SFT, prompt-token labels are set to {{c1::-100}} so loss is computed only on the {{c2::response}}.
Unbiased pass@k from n samples with c correct: pass@k = {{c1::1 - C(n-c,k)/C(n,k)}}.
Best-of-n accuracy with a perfect verifier is {{c1::1 - (1-p)^n}}.
Mean Reciprocal Rank = {{c1::1/rank}} of the first relevant result (0 if none).
The standard error of an accuracy estimate is {{c1::sqrt(p(1-p)/n)}}.
A bootstrap CI resamples per-item results with replacement and takes the {{c1::2.5}} and {{c2::97.5}} percentiles.
Expected Calibration Error measures the gap between predicted {{c1::confidence}} and empirical {{c2::accuracy}}.
KV-cache size = 2 x layers x {{c1::kv_heads}} x head_dim x {{c2::seq}} x batch x bytes.
Speculative decoding accepts a draft token with probability {{c1::min(1, p_target/p_draft)}}, else resamples from the {{c2::residual}}.
A decode step has arithmetic intensity ~ {{c1::1}} FLOP/byte, making it {{c2::memory}}-bound.
Lower-bound decode latency per token ~ {{c1::weight bytes / HBM bandwidth}}.
Naive int8 weight quantization: scale = {{c1::max|W|/127}}, q = round(W / scale).
MQA uses {{c1::one}} KV head and GQA uses a {{c2::few}} groups; both shrink the KV cache by the head ratio.
int4 weights move ~ {{c1::4}}x fewer bytes than fp16, giving up to {{c1::4}}x faster bandwidth-bound decode.
Reciprocal Rank Fusion scores a document as the sum of {{c1::1/(k + rank)}} across retrievers.
Self-consistency samples N chain-of-thought answers and returns the {{c1::majority vote}}.
An MoE routes each token to the top-{{c1::k}} experts, so per-token FLOPs scale with {{c2::k}}, not the total expert count.
The MoE auxiliary {{c1::load-balancing}} loss prevents expert {{c2::collapse}}.
RoPE position interpolation scales positions by {{c1::train_len / target_len}} before applying RoPE.
Lost-in-the-middle is a {{c1::U-shaped}} recall curve: models use the {{c2::start}} and {{c3::end}} of long context better than the middle.
CLIP is trained with a contrastive {{c1::InfoNCE}} objective on matched image-text pairs.
An {{c1::induction head}} finds a prior occurrence of the current token and predicts what {{c2::followed}} it.
{{c1::Indirect}} prompt injection plants instructions in third-party content like web pages, PDFs, or retrieved documents.
An agentic RAG app's top OWASP-LLM risks: {{c1::prompt injection}} (LLM01), {{c2::insecure output handling}} (LLM02), and {{c3::excessive agency}} (LLM08).
The principle of {{c1::least privilege}} limits an agent's tools to the minimal scoped permissions needed.
Never run model output as {{c1::SQL/shell/eval}}; instead validate, escape, and {{c2::parameterize}} it.
In LLM serving, prefill is {{c1::compute}}-bound and sets TTFT, while decode is {{c2::memory-bandwidth}}-bound and sets TPOT.
Decode at batch 1 has arithmetic intensity about {{c1::1}}, so it is {{c2::memory}}-bound; batching B raises intensity toward {{c3::B}}.
{{c1::Continuous}} (in-flight) batching lets finished sequences exit and queued ones join every step -- the biggest {{c2::throughput}} lever.
The KV cache grows with context x batch x layers x {{c1::KV-heads}}, so it -- not the weights -- caps {{c2::batch size}}.
Speculative decoding accepts a drafted token with probability {{c1::min(1, p_t/p_d)}} and resamples rejects from the residual.
{{c1::PagedAttention}} stores the KV cache in non-contiguous pages with a block table, removing {{c2::fragmentation}} and enabling prefix sharing.
RAG access control must be enforced at {{c1::retrieval}}, never at {{c2::generation}}, because the model summarizes anything in its context.
Hybrid retrieval fuses dense and sparse rankings with Reciprocal Rank Fusion: score = sum of {{c1::1/(k+rank)}}.
Evaluate RAG in two stages: retrieval with {{c1::recall@k}} and generation with {{c2::faithfulness}}.
Upgrading the embedding model invalidates every vector, so you re-index with a {{c1::dual-index}} build and an atomic {{c2::swap}}.
Design ChatGPT: the unit of work is a stateful, multi-second, {{c1::GPU}}-bound token {{c2::stream}}.
History beyond the context window is handled with recent turns verbatim plus a rolling {{c1::summary}} plus retrieved {{c2::memory}} chunks.
